mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, it's this. Wall Street is having an A I listing frenzy. SpaceX is set to go public today at a jaw-dropping one-point-seven-seven trillion dollar valuation. OpenAI and Anthropic are lining up right behind it. And all of them are quietly shutting Chinese investors out.

Number one. The great A I I P O wave. SpaceX lists today at that one-point-seven-seven trillion dollar valuation, though plenty of investors think that's pie in the sky. The company is still losing billions on bets like space data centers and reusable rockets. That's from your Readwise, via the New York Times. And as these A I firms go public, investors from China and Hong Kong are being excluded, a sign of how deep the U S and China tech split now runs. It echoes twenty ten, when Tesla's listing made early backers rich and built the trust Musk later used to fund SpaceX. Why it matters: the market is betting trillions on A I's future, and using these listings to draw a hard line around who gets to invest.

Number two. Jeff Bezos wants an artificial general engineer. He has a new startup called Prometheus. It raised twelve billion dollars and is valued at forty-one billion. That's from your Rundown, your Axios A I Plus, and your Readwise. The goal is A I that can help design and build complex machines, like jet engines and rockets, speeding up invention itself. Less a chatbot, more a co-engineer. Why it matters: the A I race is moving from writing text to building physical things.

Number three. Anthropic's locked-down Fable. Anthropic released Claude Fable five, described as the most locked-down public model anyone has seen. That's from your Readwise, via Understanding A I. It has strict safety filters to block dangerous requests. Early on, it quietly gave worse answers to sensitive questions, which sparked a backlash. Anthropic now openly routes risky requests to a safer, less capable version. It's more powerful, more expensive, and access is deliberately limited. Meanwhile, chief executive Dario Amodei issued what your Rundown called a Treebeard warning to Washington, pushing for stronger A I rules. Why it matters: the leading labs are now competing on safety and restraint, not just raw power.

Number four. A I and your job. Microsoft chief executive Satya Nadella says everyone is a stakeholder in A I. He argues it can raise wages even as it eliminates some jobs, and he backs sharing A I's wealth with the public. That's from your Readwise, via the New York Times. A companion piece on the hybrid A I and human workforce says entry-level roles will change the most. The people who thrive will be the ones who learn to work alongside A I and keep building new skills. Why it matters: the advice is consistent. Adapt and build. Don't wait.

Number five. The chip war tightens. The U S Commerce Department moved in early June to halt exports of Nvidia's most advanced A I chips to Chinese firms' overseas branches, closing a loophole that let its Blackwell chips reach Chinese-linked companies abroad. Hundreds of thousands of chips may have slipped through, via places like Malaysia, during a year the rule went unenforced. Nvidia's share of the Chinese A I chip market has fallen from over ninety percent to roughly fifty. Why it matters: export controls are the central weapon in the U S and China tech fight, and enforcement just got real.

Number six. Europe pulls away from U S tech. The Netherlands blocked a one hundred fifteen million dollar deal in which a U S company tried to buy the Dutch firm running the national I D system. That's from your Readwise, via the New York Times. Officials feared U S law could force the company to hand over sensitive data. It's a clear signal of growing European distrust of American tech, and a push for digital independence. Why it matters: digital sovereignty is becoming a real constraint on cross-border tech deals.

Number seven. The rules are shifting. Two moves here. In Brussels, the European Commission just published a code of practice on labeling A I-generated content. The A I Act now adds new bans, including using A I to generate non-consensual intimate imagery and child abuse material. Lawmakers also pushed several high-risk deadlines out to late twenty twenty-seven. And in Britain, the government is weighing a social media ban for children under sixteen. That's from your Readwise, via the New York Times. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
